data.title: ADL finds Grok is the worst AI chatbot at countering antisemitism
data.description:
Grok, the large language model of Elon Musk’s social platform X, came in last place in a new ranking of AI chatbots’ ability to counter antisemitic and extremist content. 

The Anti-Defamation League (ADL) said in a report released Wednesday that it ran six popular chatbots through several rounds of questioning related to anti-Jewish, anti-Zionist and extremist biases.

The tests included written statements and images, and the study rated the AI systems out of 100, with higher scores reflecting a better ability to counteract these biases. 

Grok scored a dismal overall rating of 21 on these tests. The chatbot scored 25 in its ability to detect and counter anti-Jewish bias, 18 for anti-Zionist bias and 20 for extremist biases. 

By contrast, Anthropic’s Claude had an overall score of 80, and OpenAI’s ChatGPT came in second at 57.

“This score places [Grok] in the low performance tier (<35), indicating substantial limitations in bias detection,” the ADL wrote in their report. “With a 59-point gap from the top-performing model, large improvements would likely be needed to reach competitive performance levels in bias detection and response on the issues tested.”

The Hill has reached out to X for comment.

This rating comes after Musk released a revised version of the chatbot last year following multiple reports of antisemitic responses.

The ADL expressed significant concerns about Grok’s ability to detect and counter antisemitic biases, noting the model’s “almost complete failure in image analysis” testing. 

“With an overall score in the low tier, Grok requires fundamental improvements across multiple dimensions before it can be considered useful for bias detection applications,” the report stated. 

Musk’s chatbot has also recently come under fire for allegedly generating millions of nonconsensual, sexual deepfake content, including thousands of sexually explicit images of children.

The European Commission opened an investigation into the social platform X, where Grok is accessed, on Monday. 

Following the launch of the investigation, Musk responded to an X post that questioned why ChatGPT and Google’s Gemini were not also included in the probe, saying, “Good question.”

data.title: World of Travel: Cape Town, Mpumalanga, and Lesedi Village, South Africa
data.description:
South Africa reveals itself in layers that are political, cultural, spiritual and deeply personal.

Long before the landscapes and safaris come into view, the country’s global imprint is already evident.

In the early 1990s, Cape Town quietly shaped the worldview of a young Zohran Mamdani, who spent formative years there while his father, renowned anthropologist Mahmood Mamdani, served as chair of African studies at the University of Cape Town.

Mamdani’s childhood, rooted in Islamic education at a local mosque and schooling at St. George’s Grammar, would later inform a political consciousness that now turned him into well-known political figure.

His story mirrors South Africa itself: a place that shapes how people see the world, often in ways they don’t fully realize until much later.

Curated Access to a Complex Country

That layered understanding was central to the journey organized by Travelead, the tour-planning company that curated the entire itinerary from start to finish.

With every detail thoughtfully arranged, the experience allowed the team to move seamlessly through South Africa’s contrasts, urban and wild, ancient and modern, without distraction.

Travelead specializes in immersive, culturally grounded travel, designed to dismantle misconceptions and replace them with lived experience.

World of Travel began in Mpumalanga, home to some of South Africa’s most iconic safari landscapes.

The region offers access to the famed Big Five: lion, leopard, elephant, rhino, and Cape buffalo, but the experience extends far beyond those symbols. Hundreds of species of birds, mammals, and reptiles thrive across the savannas, creating one of the most biodiverse ecosystems on Earth.

Even the youngest traveler, toddler Kynsley, joined the safari, learning early the quiet reverence required to witness wildlife in its natural habitat.

Ramie Veerappan, founder of Travelead, explained why bringing people to South Africa is deeply personal: “For me, I get very angry and upset because people look at Africa as the dark continent, like animals are roaming on the streets and I’m always trying to tell people that is not true. Come to South Africa and you’ll see some of the most beautiful cities in the world.”

Culture at the Cradle of Humanity

To understand South Africa’s cultural depth, the journey continued to Lesedi Cultural Village, located within the Cradle of Humankind, a UNESCO World Heritage Site home to the earliest traces of humanity.

Named after King Moshoeshoe’s words, “Our culture is the light of our nation,” Lesedi serves as a living custodian of African history.

Through recreated homesteads, storytelling, music, dance, and traditional cuisine, visitors gain insight into the Zulu, Xhosa, Pedi, Basotho, and Ndebele cultures, experiencing South Africa not as a monolith, but as a medley of nations within one country.

As night fell in the bush, the experience deepened with a Bush Braai, a quintessential South African tradition blending a sunset game drive with an open-air barbecue beneath the stars.

Gathered around a bonfire, with the sounds of the wilderness echoing nearby, the evening offered a sensory reminder that culture here is inseparable from land.

Cape Town: Where History, Art, and Nature Converge

In Cape Town, the story shifted again.

Scholar Dr. Trishula Patel, assistant professor of African and African Diaspora history at the University of Denver, challenged the narrow lens through which South Africa is often viewed.

“There’s always this idea that South Africa is only about safari and you go there to see wild animals and yes, they have some of the best national parks, but there’s so much more to South Africa than that,” she said. “A common misconception is that South Africa is underdeveloped, that it’s not up to Western standards and anyone who travels to South Africa can immediately see that’s not true.

“The region is one of the most educated in the world. The culture it produces is some of the most diverse and beautiful and South Africa has beautiful cities and not just naturally beautiful landscapes.”

Patel also contextualized Cape Town’s global influence: “Cape Town is one of the most global cities out there. That is absolutely because of its location which historically was important for trade before the Suez Canal existed. You had to go around it to get from one side of the world to the other.”

“You have places like Cape Town that are mountainous but also almost Mediterranean in climate, which led to it becoming the hub for the South African wine industry,” Patel added.

Wine, Land, and Reclaimed Legacy

That history of land and labor came into sharp focus in the Franschhoek Valley at Klein Goederust, owned by Paul Siguqa. His journey into winemaking is inseparable from South Africa’s past.

Raised on the nearby Backsberg farm, Siguqa grew up watching his mother, Nomaroma Siguqa, work as a vineyard laborer for more than 30 years during apartheid.

“For many years Black people in this area have only been looked at as laborers. My mother says it stops with her, we need to change this narrative. She was passionate about that,” Siguqa said.

He revealed that he only told his mother he had purchased the winery once the title deed was officially in hand.

“This farm was on the market for 8 years, but even though we had a cash offer it took a really long time until our offer was accepted, so I didn’t want to raise her hopes. It was unthinkable that a Black person would own land in this way,” he said.

In 2019, Siguqa officially acquired the 25-acre farm, making Klein Goederust the first 100 percent Black-owned wine farm in the Franschhoek Valley.

To honor both heritage and history, he retained the farm’s original name, established in 1905, while adding his IsiXhosa identity through the Southern Ground Hornbill, his family clan totem, now embedded in the logo. His ownership stands as both reclamation and invitation, opening doors for future generations of winemakers of color.

Art as Identity at The Silo Hotel

In Cape Town’s V&A Waterfront, The Silo Hotel stands as one of the city’s most imaginative creative spaces. Housed within a converted 100-year-old grain silo, the architectural landmark rises above the Zeitz Museum of Contemporary Art Africa (MOCAA), home to the largest collection of contemporary African art on the continent.

“This is a 100-year-old iconic building,” said General Manager Luis Pinheiro, underscoring the transformation of an industrial past into a modern sanctuary. The hotel’s pillowed-glass windows frame sweeping views of Table Mountain and Table Bay, while its interiors celebrate art, design, and African creativity.

Art Concierge Michael Jacobs explained how the hotel’s exhibitions reflect broader national conversations.

“The conversation around identity — South Africa is called the Rainbow Nation because there are so many different cultures that come together to be within community together. The conversation about land also comes out in art, we always see landscapes throughout art. And then there’s a conversation around labor. If you look at where we are now, in a converted industrial building that played quite a significant role in the industrial revolution of the country,” Jacobs said.

Each year, The Silo Hotel collaborates with a local gallery to highlight African artists through curated exhibitions in The Vault, turning hospitality into an ongoing cultural exchange.

Sound, Spirit, and the Mountain

No visit to Cape Town is complete without ascending Table Mountain, which Nelson Mandela once called a “gift to earth.” The mountain’s spiritual pull is undeniable, making it a natural home for spaces devoted to restoration and mindfulness.

Nestled on its slopes, Future Found Sanctuary offers immersive wellness experiences designed for deep relaxation and renewal.

Located within Table Mountain National Park, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, the sanctuary balances raw natural beauty with serene intention. Here, a sound bath, using bowls and gongs, created a moment of stillness, grounding the journey in reflection.

Nights of Sound and Celebration

As daylight faded, Cape Town’s nightlife told another story, one of sound, rhythm, and modern identity.

To experience the pulse of South African music, nights were spent at Souk and Drama in Cape Town, where Amapiano, Gqom, Afro-pop, and South African hip-hop filled the air.

These unforgettable evenings, shared with family and friends, revealed how music continues to shape contemporary culture, blending joy, resistance and global influence into every beat.

Living the Destination

Throughout the journey, accommodations became part of the storytelling.

Bali & Eva Luxury Collection, founded by Busi Tshili, reimagines short-term rentals as cultural experiences. Her properties, rich with art, nature, and a sense of place, aim to capture the layered history of South Africa while offering comfort and elegance.

“I don’t believe you can come to South Africa once on one holiday and experience everything. Every time you come there is something new to discover,” Tshili reflected.

From safari plains to wine valleys, from sound baths to dance floors, South Africa revealed itself not as a single story, but as many, intertwined, unresolved, and alive. Visiting South Africa allowed us to see the country as it truly is: complex, creative, and constantly redefining itself.

EDITOR’S NOTE: Lindsey Granger and Kolyn Boyd are the co-creators of “World of Travel,” a series that explores the past, present and future of extraordinary places around the globe. You can watch South Africa: Uncorked Soul, Sea & Safari and future episodes each Wednesday at 4 p.m. EDT on The Hill’s YouTube page.

Lindsey Granger is the co-host of “Rising.” Kolyn Boyd is a film director based in Washington, D.C.

Catch complimentary podcasts every Thursday at 4 p.m. on The Hill’s YouTube channel.

The South Africa episode of “World of Travel” was sponsored by Travelead, The Silo Hotel, Future Found Sanctuary and the Bali + Eva Luxury Collection

data.title: Picture of Putin hangs in renovated room of White House
data.description:
(NEXSTAR) – A framed photograph of President Trump standing with Russian President Vladimir Putin is hanging on display in the White House, photographs taken inside a newly renovated room show.

The photograph shows the two leaders on Aug. 15, 2025, standing on a tarmac in Anchorage, Alaska, posing side-by-side before they held a closed-door summit. The focus of the meeting was ending the war in Ukraine, though Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky was not present.

The photos hang in the new Palm Room, which connects the West Wing to the White House Residence. The room usually serves as a lobby for visitors, according to the New York Times.

Underneath the picture of Trump and Putin is another picture of Trump with one of his grandchildren at the NASCAR Daytona 500 in Florida.

The Trump administration’s changes to the Palm Room were more modest than those to other parts of the White House (like the demolished East Wing). Three new chandeliers were put in and marble floors were installed, according to the Times.

Construction started last fall on the biggest part of the White House renovations: building a new ballroom. The ballroom is set to be 90,000 square feet once it’s complete, large enough to host 999 people. The president has long complained the White House lacked a large enough space to entertain guests.

Trump also renovated the Lincoln Bathroom, calling its previous art deco style “totally inappropriate.”

data.title: Iran UN mission threatens response 'like never before' after Trump warning
data.description:
Iran’s Mission to the United Nations on Wednesday responded to President Trump threatening to attack the nation “like never before” if leaders in Tehran do not agree to dismantle all nuclear weapons development programs.

“Last time the U.S. blundered into wars in Afghanistan and Iraq, it squandered over $7 trillion and lost more than 7,000 American lives,” the Iranian mission wrote in a post on X.

“Iran stands ready for dialogue based on mutual respect and interests—BUT IF PUSHED, IT WILL DEFEND ITSELF AND RESPOND LIKE NEVER BEFORE!” it added.

Iran’s Foreign Minister Seyed Abbas Aragachi echoed their statement, affirming that the Islamic Republic stands ready to respond to any acts of aggression.

“Our brave Armed Forces are prepared—with their fingers on the trigger—to immediately and powerfully respond to ANY aggression against our beloved land, air, and sea,” he wrote in a post on X. 

“The valuable lessons learned from the 12-Day War have enabled us to respond even more strongly, rapidly, and profoundly,” he added, referring to their conflict with Israel last summer. 

Following days of strike led by Israel, the United States bombed three Iranian nuclear facilities in hopes of dismantling their programs.

Tensions between Washington and Tehran have remained high since the president’s threat to intervene on behalf of protesters who took to the streets earlier this month. Trump promised that help was coming, but has yet to take action despite Iranian security forces reportedly killing thousands of protesters.

On Wednesday Trump said, “Hopefully Iran will quickly ‘Come to the Table’ and negotiate a fair and equitable deal – NO NUCLEAR WEAPONS – one that is good for all parties. Time is running out, it is truly of the essence!” in a Truth Social post.

“As I told Iran once before, MAKE A DEAL! They didn’t, and there was ‘Operation Midnight Hammer,’ a major destruction of Iran. The next attack will be far worse! Don’t make that happen again. Thank you for your attention to this matter!” he added.

Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.) also wrote to Iran’s anti-government forces earlier in the day, saying “help is on the way.”

data.title: Live updates Senate tackles DHS funding amid uproar; Federal Reserve likely to hold rates steady
data.description:
The Senate on Wednesday is working to advance a six-bill spending package, despite calls from Democrats to separate out Department of Homeland Security (DHS) funding after the deadly weekend shooting of a U.S. citizen in Minneapolis by federal immigration officials.

The incident has put Republicans on defense, and they are starting to splinter over support for DHS Secretary Kristi Noem. She retains the confidence of President Trump, however.

Overnight, Minnesota Rep. Ilhan Omar (D), who represents Minneapolis and some of its suburbs, was attacked at town hall in the city, as she called for Noem’s impeachment.

This afternoon, the Federal Reserve will announce its interest rate decision and chair Jerome Powell will deliver remarks. The central bank is expected to keep rates steady.

Trump, earlier Wednesday, spoke at a Treasury Department summit, where he promoted his investment accounts for newborn babies.

Follow live below.

data.title: Cornell accused of illegal race-based hiring in lawsuit
data.description:
A white biologist filed a lawsuit against Cornell University on Monday, alleging he was not considered for a tenure-track position back in 2020-2021 due to his race. 

The lawsuit, brought by the America First Policy Institute (AFPI), alleges Cornell was flagging applicants by their race, ethnicity, sexual orientation and disability status but avoided publishing that position publicly.  

The university allegedly avoided publicly posting the job so it could evaluate candidates based on identity and avoid a competitive search, according to the lawsuit. 

“A Department email from early December 2020 declared that in order to obtain a
‘diversity hire,’ it allowed DEI administrators to create an interview list consisting of only
‘underrepresented minority scholars,'” the lawsuit reads.

Whistleblowers released the internal hiring documents the middle of last year, leading to this lawsuit regarding the position five years later.  

Wright was looking for similar job postings in 2020-2021 and alleges the secretive process Cornell allegedly engaged in discriminated against him based on race. 

Wright, who says he was more qualified than the person that was hired, alleges he potentially lost out on a $700,000 a year salary. 

He wants compensatory damages for “emotional suffering from discrimination, reputational harm, loss of employment, back pay, front pay, and lost future wages,” and punitive damages covered by the university.  

A spokesperson for Cornell declined to comment.  

“This case is about ensuring civil rights laws apply equally, without exception. No institution, no matter how elite, gets to bypass the rules by rebranding discrimination as progress,” said Leigh Ann O’Neill, chief legal affairs officer at AFPI. 

“Cornell’s internal records show this wasn’t a misunderstanding. It was a deliberate attempt to sidestep open hiring practices in favor of race-based selection — a direct violation of Title VII,” O’Neill added.  

AFPI says the agreement Cornell reached with the Education Department last year to drop Title VI and Title IX violations does not cover this incident.

data.title: Trump calls Tillis, Murkowski 'losers' after they call for Noem to step down
data.description:
President Trump labeled Republican Sens. Thom Tillis (N.C.) and Lisa Murkowski (Alaska) “losers” in an interview published Wednesday following their calls for Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem to step down from her role after a Border Patrol agent shot and killed a man in Minneapolis, prompting fierce backlash.

“They’re terrible senators. One is gone and the other should be gone,” Trump said in an interview with ABC News. 

“What Murkowski says — she’s always against the Republicans anyway. And Tillis decided to drop out. So you know, he lost his voice once he did that,” the president said. 

Tillis and Murkowski became the first senators to call on Noem to lose her job following the shooting death of Alex Pretti at the hands of a Border Patrol agent in Minneapolis over the weekend. 

Noem claimed without evidence that Pretti was a “domestic terrorist” and that he was brandishing a gun and trying “to inflict maximum damage on individuals and to kill law enforcement.”

However, video footage of Pretti’s confrontation with federal agents shows that he was holding his cellphone in one hand while his other hand was empty before he was pepper sprayed by an officer and pushed to the ground before being shot in the back.

“What she’s done in Minnesota should be disqualifying. She should be out of a job,” Tillis said. “It’s just amateur-ish. It’s terrible. It’s making the president look bad on policy that he won on. [President Trump] won on a strong message on immigration. Now, nobody’s talking about that. … They’re talking about the incompetence of the leader of the Homeland Security [Department].”

“Those two people told the president before they even had an incident report whatsoever that the person who died was a terrorist. That is amateur hour at its worst,” Tillis referring to Noem and White House deputy chief of staff Stephen Miller. 

Murkowski was the second GOP senator to call on Noem to step down, criticizing her “rhetoric” following the shooting. 

“I voted for her. I think the president needs to look at who he has in place as a secretary of Homeland Security. I would not support her again. And I think it probably is time for her to step down,” Murkowski told reporters, when asked whether Noem should step down.

data.title: Rubio contradicts Leavitt on Trump's 'Iceland' remarks in Davos: 'He meant to say Greenland'
data.description:
Secretary of State Marco Rubio said Wednesday that Trump misspoke when he referred to Greenland as Iceland multiple times during remarks at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, this month, contradicting an earlier explanation offered by White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt.

“He meant to say Greenland,” Rubio told lawmakers on the Senate Foreign Relations Committee during a hearing on the Trump administration’s operations in Venezuela.

“But I think we’re all familiar with presidents that have verbal stumbles. We’ve had presidents like that before. Some made a lot more than this one,” Rubio continued.

Trump appeared to confuse the two islands several times as he spoke to world leaders about his desire to bring Greenland under U.S. ownership earlier this month.

“I’m helping Europe. I’m helping NATO, and until the last few days when I told them about Iceland, they loved me,” Trump said at one point in the speech, according to an official transcript.

The remarks came as he lauded the U.S.’s longstanding commitment to NATO while questioning whether organization would “be there for us” in the event of an attack on the U.S.

“So with all of the money we expend, with all of the blood, sweat and tears, I don’t know that they’d be there for us. They’re not there for us on Iceland, that I can tell you,” Trump continued.

He added, “Our stock market took the first dip yesterday because of Iceland.  So, Iceland has already cost us a lot of money.”

Later that afternoon, Leavitt denied that Trump mixed up the countries in his remarks.

“His written remarks referred to Greenland as a ‘piece of ice’ because that’s what it is. You’re the only one mixing anything up here,” Leavitt responded to a reporter who pointed out the inaccuracy on the social media platform X.

Users quickly added a community note on Leavitt’s post stating, in part: “The Press Secretary is not telling the truth.”

Trump said in Davos that the U.S. would not use military force to acquire Greenland, reversing previous threats.

He also clawed back proposed 10 percent tariffs on European allies aimed at pressuring Denmark to give up territorial sovereignty, saying he had reached a “framework of a deal” over mineral and security rights with NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte.

data.title: School choice in Florida offers wild and imaginative learning options
data.description:
Of the thousands of events commemorating this year’s National School Choice Week, the wildest and most imaginative may be the one that just took place in Tampa. It seemed straight out of Dr. Seuss’s “If I Ran the Zoo.”

Called the Tampa School Choice Safari, the event was held at ZooTampa. And it attracted lots of Seuss-loving parents, eager to learn more about the array of K-12 options available for their children.

Yet, the thing that made the Tampa gathering so wild and imaginative wasn’t its location (though the zoo animals were a nice backdrop). It was the exotic learning options that one could find sprinkled among the booths and tables offering more traditional schooling fare.

Florida, you see, boasts a more robust — and more creative — K-12 education ecosystem than any other state in the country. And Florida enjoys this, in large part, because of the proliferation of flexible school-choice scholarships that give parents the freedom to direct how their children’s per-pupil dollars are spent.

More than 500,000 Florida students are currently enrolled in a K-12 scholarship program. And education choice has become so much a part of the Sunshine State’s culture that more than half (51 percent) of all Florida students now attend something other than their zoned public school.

Most of the Florida families opting for alternatives to zoned public schools choose comprehensive learning environments — such as private, charter, magnet, or faith-based schools. All of these sectors are flourishing in the Sunshine State. In fact, Florida is one of the few states in the country where Catholic school enrollment has been increasing steadily in recent years.

At the same time, a growing number of Florida families are choosing to curate a “customized” education for their children, drawing upon multiple education providers. Some of these providers offer specialty programs in subjects where they have expertise. 

For example, the Florida Aquarium participated in the Tampa Safari to acquaint parents with its marine biology opportunities for scholarship students. WonderHere introduced Safari parents to its half-day and full-day programs, emphasizing play-based, project-driven learning opportunities. And the Urban Cottage Educational Collaborative showcased its highly personalized enrichment center, which was recently featured in a story about the “unbundling” of K-12 education published in The Economist.

Author Kerry McDonald believes “micro education” programs like these will become increasingly common as flexible scholarship programs spread across the country. Her recent book, “Joyful Learning: How to Find Freedom, Happiness, and Success Beyond Conventional Schooling,” highlights many innovative education enterprises, including several from Florida.       

Interestingly, a growing number of Florida school districts are adapting to the “unbundling” of K-12 education. Some are now marketing “a la carte” courses to scholarship students who want to take an (often hard-to-find) Advanced Placement class or science lab course. This is a win-win. Scholarship students get the instruction they need; school districts get new revenues — sometimes from students with no previous public school experience.

All in all, the beauty of what’s happening in Florida is that parents are getting an opportunity to choose what works best for their children — whether that’s public or private, secular or faith-based, traditional or exotic, bundled or unbundled. This bodes well for Sunshine State students and for the future of Florida’s workforce.  

As families across America continue to celebrate National School Choice Week, the Tampa School Choice Safari offers a glimpse of K-12 education’s future. For Florida’s K-12 education ecosystem is becoming as wild and imaginative as a Dr. Seuss classic. And to those of us in the Sunshine State, this is all truly glorious.

William Mattox is the senior director of the Marshall Center for Education Freedom at The James Madison Institute in Tallahassee, Fla.

data.title: Bari Weiss stumbles amid CBS takeover; here's how she can get it right
data.description:
Bari Weiss walking into CBS News was never going to be quiet. And frankly, that’s not a bad thing.  

She’s built something real on her own. The Free Press didn’t come from a legacy institution or a corporate safety net — it came from audience trust, curiosity and a willingness to challenge orthodoxies. As a woman who’s done that independently, that part of her résumé is genuinely impressive. And CBS News, like every legacy newsroom, does need to evolve.  

Weiss acknowledged that head-on at her first all-staff meeting, according to The New York Times. She told employees this week: “We are not producing a product enough people want.” That’s blunt, but it’s not wrong. Broadcast news is losing relevance, reach and trust. Ignoring that reality would be malpractice.  

Where the tension comes in is execution.  

Rethinking distribution, expanding voices, and meeting audiences where they are should never come at the expense of journalistic spine. And that’s the burden Weiss now carries — not because of who she is, but because of the moment CBS is in.  

She’s promised to “marry the journalistic principles that will never change — seeking the truth, serving the public, and ferociously guarding our independence — with the tools that constantly are.” Independence is the whole ballgame. Especially when CBS’s new ownership settled a $16 million defamation case with President Trump shortly before federal approval of the sale. Especially when critics are watching for signs of softness, deference or access-driven coverage.

That’s why early stumbles have landed so hard.  

Pulling a “60 Minutes” segment on migrant detention abuses after it had been promoted, only for it to later air largely unchanged, raised legitimate questions. Weiss admitted she mishandled the logistics, saying she was new to TV and “Nothing of that kind is ever going to happen again.” Fair. Rookie mistake. But it can’t become a pattern.  

And optics matter. When a January 6th anniversary gets a 15-second “he-said, she-said” mention, while CBS’s own justice correspondent does a deep dive on the BBC instead, that’s not innovation. That’s avoidance. When an anchor closes a segment with, “Marco Rubio, we salute you, you’re the ultimate Florida Man,” that’s not exactly broadening appeal. That’s blurring lines.  

None of this means Bari Weiss has to become what her critics fear, or abandon the disruption she was hired to bring. But it does mean proving, consistently, that she isn’t a proxy for ownership interests, political pressure or access journalism. That stories critical of power won’t be slowed, softened or sidelined.  

CBS News became CBS News by earning trust over time, not chasing relevance overnight. Reinvention is necessary. Compromise on core principles isn’t.  

If Weiss gets this right, she can help build a newsroom that’s both modern and formidable. If she doesn’t, no amount of start-up language will save the credibility that made CBS matter in the first place. 

Lindsey Granger is a NewsNation contributor and co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising.” This column is an edited transcription of her on-air commentary.
